content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA of length NNN.

You can perform the following operation on it:


Choose any contiguous subarray of AAA such that its length is **greater than 111** and **odd**.
Then, delete the median†^\dagger† of this subarray from it.

If there are multiple occurrences of the median within the subarray, delete the **leftmost** one.
Note that the length of the array decreases by 111 after the operation.

For example, if the array is A=[1,3,1,4,1,5]A = [1, 3, 1, 4, 1, 5]A=[1,3,1,4,1,5] and you choose the subarray [1,4,1][1, 4,1][1,4,1] from indices 333 to 555, the median is 111.

The leftmost occurrence of 111 within the subarray is at index 333, so it will be deleted and the array becomes [1,3,4,1,5][1, 3, 4, 1, 5][1,3,4,1,5].

Is it possible to perform this operation several times (possibly, zero) so that the final array AAA is sorted in **non-descending order**?

If it is possible, also find any valid sequence of operations.

Note that you do not need to minimize the number of operations: you only need to find any valid sequence.

†^\dagger† The median of an odd-length array is the middle element when the array is written in **sorted** order.

For example, if A=[3,1,4,1,5]A = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]A=[3,1,4,1,5], the median is 333 since the sorted array is [1,1,3,4,5][1, 1, 3, 4, 5][1,1,3,4,5].


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of input contains a single integer NNN, the length of the array AAA.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.




Output Format
For each test case,



If it is not possible to obtain a sorted array no matter what, print a single line containing the integer −1-1−1.
Otherwise,

First, print a single line containing one integer KKK: the number of operations you wish to perform.
The next KKK lines should describe the operations you perform.

On the iii-th of these KKK lines, print two space-separated integers LiL_iLi​ and RiR_iRi​, meaning that the operation is performed on the subarray [ALi,ALi+1,…,ARi][A_{L_i}, A_{L_i+1}, \ldots, A_{R_i}][ALi​​,ALi​+1​,…,ARi​​] of the current array AAA.



These integers should satisfy 1≤Li<Ri≤N−i+11 \leq L_i \lt R_i \leq N-i+11≤Li​<Ri​≤N−i+1, and also Ri−Li+1R_i - L_i + 1Ri​−Li​+1 should be odd.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 500050005000.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Consider the following sequence of operations:

Choose the subarray [4,1,4][4, 1, 4][4,1,4], from indices 222 to 444. The median is 444, deleting its leftmost occurrence results in A=[3,1,4,5]A = [3, 1, 4, 5]A=[3,1,4,5].
Choose the subarray [3,1,4][3, 1, 4][3,1,4] from indices 111 to 333. The median is 333, deleting it results in
A=[1,4,5]A = [1, 4, 5]A=[1,4,5] which is sorted.

Test case 222: AAA cannot be sorted by performing the given operation.
